faculty.title: Professor
faculty.research_interests: Marine ecology, conservation biology, fisheries, coral reefs, marine protected areas
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Octavio Aburto‑Oropeza’s research integrates marine ecology and conservation biology to evaluate and improve the effectiveness of marine protected areas, fisheries management, and coastal habitat restoration. He focuses on mangrove, kelp forest, and coral reef ecosystems, assessing climate‑change impacts, blue‑carbon dynamics, and ecosystem services such as fisheries productivity and tourism. His work combines ecological field data with socio‑economic analyses, including habitat protection indices and social cost of carbon calculations, to inform policy and sustainable management of marine resources.

faculty.title: Professor
faculty.research_interests: Marine microbiology, phytoplankton genomics, biogeochemistry, metagenomics
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Andrew Allen investigates marine microbial ecology with an emphasis on phytoplankton genomics, biogeochemistry, and metagenomics. His work integrates field sampling across ocean basins (e.g., Southern Ocean, South Pacific, Indian Ocean) with genome‑resolved analyses to understand harmful algal bloom dynamics, domoic acid biosynthesis, and the functional seascape of marine microbes. He also develops molecular tools such as digital PCR assays and engineered diatom pathways to enhance production of valuable polyunsaturated fatty acids.

faculty.research_interests: Physical oceanography, Southern Ocean dynamics, satellite remote sensing, air-sea interaction
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Sarah Gille investigates the physical processes that control the Southern Ocean’s role in the Earth’s climate system. Her work integrates satellite remote sensing, autonomous float observations, and high‑resolution numerical modeling to quantify air‑sea fluxes, diapycnal and isopycnal mixing, and energy transfers from winds to oceanic currents and internal waves. She also examines how variability in the Antarctic Circumpolar Current and mesoscale‑submesoscale dynamics influence heat, carbon, and water‑mass distribution on regional and global scales.

faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Jennifer MacKinnon investigates the physical processes that drive ocean mixing, turbulence, and internal‑wave dynamics across a range of spatial scales, from submesoscale vortices to basin‑wide circulations. Her work combines ship‑based, moored, and towed microstructure observations with numerical and climate‑model simulations to quantify heat and salt fluxes in regions such as the Arctic Ocean, the northern Gulf of Mexico, and the Southern Ocean. By linking theory, field data, and machine‑learning analyses, she aims to improve parameterizations of diapycnal mixing for global climate models.

faculty.research_interests_enriched: Lisa Levin investigates the ecology and biogeochemistry of deep‑sea benthic habitats, with particular emphasis on methane seeps, hydrothermal vents, and oxygen‑minimum zones on Pacific continental margins. Her work integrates field sampling with submersible/ROV deployments, experimental larval physiology, and geochemical proxy development to elucidate how extreme chemical conditions shape community structure, connectivity, and carbon cycling, and how these systems respond to climate‑driven changes such as ocean acidification and deoxygenation.

faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Ramanathan’s research centers on the physical processes that control Earth’s climate, with particular emphasis on how greenhouse gases, aerosols (especially black carbon and brown clouds) and cloud‑aerosol interactions modify radiative forcing and drive global warming. He integrates satellite, airborne (UAV) and ground‑based observations with climate and radiative‑transfer modeling to quantify regional and global climate impacts and to develop mitigation strategies for vulnerable populations that rely on biomass fuels. His work also explores climate‑water‑carbon feedbacks and the role of atmospheric chemistry in shaping climate trajectories.

faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Joel Norris investigates how clouds interact with the climate system, focusing on cloud radiative feedbacks, marine stratiform clouds, and atmospheric rivers. His work combines satellite remote sensing, climate model diagnostics, and observational analyses to quantify the impact of clouds and aerosols on surface solar radiation and climate sensitivity. He also studies decadal variability in cloud–circulation–temperature feedbacks across the global ocean, with particular emphasis on the Southern Ocean and western North America.

faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Peter Franks investigates the coupled physical‑biological dynamics of marine ecosystems, focusing on plankton and zooplankton population processes across scales from microscale patchiness to basin‑wide climate forcing. His work integrates autonomous underwater robotics, high‑resolution imaging fluorometry, and numerical modeling to understand harmful algal bloom transport, submesoscale current impacts, and the development and survival of Antarctic krill larvae. These efforts aim to improve prediction and management of coastal and open‑ocean resources under changing climate conditions.

One reason for the dustiness of the atmosphere is that atmospheric dust concentration has increased dramatically since the Industrial Revolution, possibly due to the expansion of cleared, tilled, agricultural land. funded_grants.abstract: Dust storms are ubiquitous in Earth’s atmosphere and are composed of small mineral particles having diameters of varying sizes. Theory suggests that for dust transported long distances, the diameters of these particles should be limited to approximately 10 microns or smaller, due to the downward gravitational force acting to remove larger particles from the atmosphere. funded_grants.title: Meteorological Controls on Dust Emission and Transport in a Closed Basin
funded_grants.abstract: By mass, dust is the most pervasive aerosol in the Earth's atmosphere. Dust plays an important role in the Earth's weather and climate since mineral aerosols affect the hydrological cycle, oceanic and terrestrial nutrient budget, and radiative energy budget. faculty.title: Distinguished Professor
faculty.research_interests: Climate dynamics, tropical meteorology, ocean-atmosphere interaction, climate modeling
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Shang‑Ping Xie focuses on the dynamics of the tropical climate system and its interaction with the global ocean‑atmosphere circulation. His work investigates major modes of variability such as ENSO, the Indian Ocean Dipole, the ITCZ, and monsoon systems, using climate models, satellite and shipboard observations, and emerging machine‑learning techniques to understand past changes and improve future climate predictions.

funded_grants.abstract: Every few years the Pacific ocean warms dramatically in a narrow strip along the equator extending roughly from the South American coast to the dateline.  This warming is called an El Nino event, and El Ninos and their cold La Nina opposites are referred to collectively as El Nino/Southern Oscillation (ENSO) events. funded_grants.abstract: Satellites views of the earth typically show a narrow band of deep precipitating clouds over the tropical oceans where the trade winds converge.  This band, known as the inter-tropical convergence zone, or ITCZ, is generally found north of the equator with a mean latitude of about 6N. funded_grants.abstract: Summer is the rainy season for East Asia. The Meiyu-Baiu rain band, extending from the eastern flank of the Tibetan Plateau northeastward through Korea and Japan, is the single most important climate phenomenon of the region. The termination of this summer rain band in late July has been tied to the abrupt onset of tropical convection over the Northwest Pacific east of the Philippines, through a meridional dipole pattern of summer rainfall variability. faculty.title: Professor
faculty.research_interests: Climate policy, energy transitions, international environmental policy, decarbonization
faculty.research_interests_enriched: David Victor’s research investigates the design and effectiveness of climate and energy policies at the international and sub‑national levels, emphasizing how policy sequencing, governance mechanisms, and capital mobilization can accelerate decarbonization. He integrates analysis of emerging mitigation technologies—such as clean aviation, direct‑air capture, ocean alkalinity enhancement, and enhanced‑root crops—with political‑institutional strategies like naming‑and‑shaming to drive climate action. His work bridges climate‑policy theory, quantitative modeling, and empirical case studies to inform large‑scale energy transitions and carbon‑removal pathways.

faculty.title: Professor
faculty.research_interests: Glaciology, Antarctic ice sheet, satellite remote sensing, ICESat, subglacial lakes
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Helen Fricker’s research centers on the dynamics of the Antarctic ice sheet, with particular emphasis on subglacial hydrology, ice‑ocean interactions, and the processes governing ice shelf stability. She integrates satellite remote‑sensing (ICESat/ICESat‑2 altimetry), electromagnetic and aerogeophysical surveys, and numerical modeling to map three‑dimensional water systems beneath the ice and to investigate iceberg calving, rift development, and carbon cycling in subglacial lakes. Her work also addresses the broader implications of Antarctic ice loss for sea‑level rise and climate change.

faculty.research_interests_enriched: Yui Takeshita investigates how ocean acidification and carbon cycling affect coastal and reef ecosystems, with a particular focus on seagrass meadows and coral reefs. She integrates biogeochemical modeling with the development and field deployment of advanced chemical sensors for simultaneous measurement of pH, total alkalinity, and chloride in seawater, including pressure‑tolerant designs for deep‑sea networks. Her work bridges marine chemistry, sensor engineering, and ecosystem science to improve understanding of acidification buffering capacity in temperate coastal waters.

faculty.research_interests_enriched: Anela Choy investigates the structure and function of deep‑sea and mesopelagic ecosystems, focusing on how surface and deep pelagic food webs are linked through vertical trophic pathways. faculty.title: Research Scientist
faculty.research_interests: Physical oceanography, ocean acoustics, internal waves, ocean instrumentation
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Robert Pinkel’s research centers on physical oceanography with an emphasis on internal wave dynamics, tidal mixing, and ocean acoustic processes. He develops and deploys advanced ocean instrumentation—including fiber‑optic distributed temperature sensing, hydrographic Doppler sonar, and low‑cost profiling floats—to obtain high‑resolution measurements from meters to tens of kilometers. His work spans field experiments in diverse settings such as the South China Sea, Hawaiian Ridge, Tasmanian shelf, and Arctic ice‑ocean interface, and includes validation of satellite sea‑surface‑height observations (SWOT).
methodologies: distributed temperature sensing (DTS) with optical fibers
numerical modeling of internal wave propagation

faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Falko Kuester’s research centers on the development and application of advanced visualization, virtual reality, and remote‑sensing technologies for the exploration of large, complex datasets. He integrates 3D imaging and high‑performance collaborative display systems to support cultural‑heritage diagnostics, archaeological documentation, and Earth‑system science modeling, with recent work extending to coral‑reef point‑cloud analysis. His interdisciplinary approach combines computer graphics, machine‑learning‑based segmentation, and immersive environments to enable new insights across heritage and environmental domains.
